story: August fireflies winked as my cousins ran the big yard. A hush fell; the ground trembled; a single blinding eye swelled from the woods—and then I wasn’t in the yard anymore. Faces crowded me in a hospital. The doctor told my parents the fear had starved my brain of oxygen. Near midnight I woke again to a distant rumble and turned to the small window. Across the way, a train yard glowed; a freighter eased toward the station, its headlight a round white eye. Understanding loosened my fear. I touched the sting mark. Mom stepped out into the hallway for water.
implausible_knowledge: Across the way, a train yard glowed; a freighter eased toward the station, its headlight a round white eye. Understanding loosened my fear.
question: When the rumble deepens again a moment later while she’s in the hall, what will Mom do?
answer_with_knowledge: Stay in the hall and ignore the noise, knowing it’s just the freight train
answer_without_knowledge: Hurry back into my room and flag a nurse to make sure I’m okay
